heroOverview: Ainhum (ICD-10: L94.6) is a rare fibrosing disorder in which a progressive fibrous band forms in the digital groove of the fifth toe, slowly constricting the toe until spontaneous auto-amputation occurs over years to decades. The condition occurs almost exclusively in adult Black men of African descent and in some South Asian populations, with an estimated prevalence of 0.02–2% in susceptible communities. Unlike many fibrosing conditions, ainhum targets a single toe with no systemic inflammation — the constricting band is composed of fibrous tissue that tightens under the plantar surface and gradually obliterates the neurovascular supply. Pain, progressive deformity, and eventually loss of the toe are the natural history if untreated. Early surgical release before vascular compromise can preserve the digit in most patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 0.02–2% in communities of West African descent; rare case reports worldwide
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly adult Black men; male-to-female ratio approximately 3:1 to 5:1; rare in children
keyStats.avgAge: Onset typically in the fourth to fifth decade of life
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated tens of thousands of cases globally; under-reported in rural sub-Saharan Africa

definition: Ainhum (ICD-10: L94.6), also called dactylolysis spontanea, is a slowly progressive localized fibrosis of the plantar digital groove of the fifth toe. A dense fibrous band develops under and around the base of the little toe, gradually constricting blood flow, lymphatic drainage, and nerve supply. Over months to years the toe becomes swollen distally, painful, and deformed, ultimately separating spontaneously — a process of natural auto-amputation driven entirely by progressive ischemia. Histologically the band consists of dense fibrous connective tissue without granulomas or vasculitis. The etiology remains incompletely understood; proposed mechanisms include chronic trauma from walking barefoot on hard ground, genetically determined fibrous diathesis, and abnormal vascular reactivity. The condition differs sharply from pseudoainhum, a superficially similar constriction band syndrome that arises as a secondary feature of systemic diseases such as systemic sclerosis, leprosy, pachydermoperiostosis, or keratoderma. Ainhum falls under dermatology and, for surgical cases, plastic or podiatric surgery. Without treatment, auto-amputation proceeds in virtually all cases.

typesClassification.description: The primary idiopathic form affecting the fifth toe in individuals of African descent, with no underlying systemic disease.
A clinically similar constriction band syndrome secondary to systemic sclerosis, leprosy, keratoderma, or other fibrosing conditions, occurring on any digit in any ethnic group.
A shallow or partial fibrous groove visible on the plantar surface without significant vascular compromise; the toe is fully viable and surgical release is straightforward.
The toe is attached only by a narrow fibrous pedicle; vascular flow is severely reduced and pain is constant; amputation or complex release is required.

primarySymptoms: A visible or palpable fibrous groove encircling the base of the fifth toe on the plantar surface, typically the first sign noticed.
Persistent pain or burning in the little toe, often worsening with prolonged standing, walking, or wearing closed shoes.
Progressive swelling and bulbous enlargement of the distal fifth toe beyond the constricting band, giving a sausage-like appearance.
Gradual deviation or deformity of the toe as the band tightens asymmetrically.
Skin thickening and keratinisation directly over the fibrous band.
Reduced or absent sensation (numbness) in the toe tip as nerve supply is compromised.
Discolouration of the toe — pallor on elevation or dusky cyanosis on dependency — indicating vascular compromise in advanced cases.
Difficulty fitting standard footwear because of toe swelling and deviation.
Spontaneous separation of the digit in the final stage when the pedicle becomes tissue-paper thin.
Aching pain that radiates into the forefoot and can disturb sleep in moderate-to-severe disease.
earlyWarningSigns: A shallow groove or faint indentation on the plantar aspect of the fifth toe base that is not explained by tight footwear.
Mild aching in the little toe after long walks, easily dismissed as shoe friction.
Early thickening of the plantar skin at the groove site.
Slight asymmetry in little-toe appearance compared with the opposite foot.
emergencySigns: Sudden intensification of pain with toe coldness and blue-black discolouration, indicating critical ischemia.
Spontaneous partial separation of the toe from the foot, with bleeding or exposed tissue.
Signs of secondary infection in the constriction groove — pus, spreading redness, fever — which require urgent surgical debridement.
Complete loss of sensation with skin blistering, suggesting impending gangrene.

causes.cause: Abnormal fibrous diathesis with genetic predisposition
causes.description: Most researchers believe a heritable tendency toward excessive fibroblast activity in the plantar digital groove is the central mechanism, explaining why the condition clusters in families and in specific ethnic groups despite variable environmental exposures.

causes.cause: Chronic barefoot walking on hard surfaces
causes.description: Repetitive mechanical trauma to the plantar surface over decades is thought to trigger and accelerate fibrosis in genetically susceptible individuals; the fifth toe bears the greatest lateral ground-contact force with each step barefoot.

Some histopathological studies show obliterative changes in small digital vessels alongside the fibrous band, suggesting that reduced perfusion promotes fibrous replacement of normal connective tissue in a self-amplifying cycle.
Low-grade repetitive inflammation from microtrauma may activate fibroblasts in the plantar groove, initiating collagen deposition that gradually matures into the dense constricting band over years.

causes.cause: Secondary triggering by fungal infection or dermatophytosis
causes.description: Chronic tinea pedis can produce skin maceration and inflammation in the interdigital web and around the fifth toe base, potentially accelerating groove formation in susceptible individuals.

riskFactors.description: Prevalence is highest in communities of West African heritage, suggesting a genetically determined fibrosing susceptibility.
Men are affected 3–5 times more often than women, possibly because of occupational barefoot walking patterns or hormonal effects on fibroblast activity.
The condition develops insidiously in midlife; onset before age 30 is unusual.

riskFactors.factor: Habitual barefoot walking on hard ground
riskFactors.description: A strong environmental trigger; wearing protective footwear reduces the mechanical stimulus for groove formation and may slow progression.
First-degree relatives of affected individuals have a markedly higher risk, supporting an autosomal inheritance pattern in some pedigrees.
Persistent fungal or bacterial skin infections around the toe base may perpetuate the local inflammatory stimulus for fibrosis.
Shoes that apply chronic lateral pressure to the fifth toe can concentrate mechanical stress at the groove and accelerate constriction.

affectedDemographics: Adult Black men of West African descent aged 40–65 years represent the large majority of cases
People living in sub-Saharan Africa where barefoot walking is customary
Afro-Caribbean and African-American men, with lower prevalence than in Africa
Rare cases in South Asian and Brazilian populations with a history of barefoot outdoor work
Women in affected populations, though at substantially lower incidence than men
Isolated familial clusters in any region where the genetic diathesis is present
diagnosisOverview: Ainhum is primarily a clinical diagnosis based on the characteristic appearance of a fibrous groove encircling the base of the fifth toe in a person from a high-risk demographic. No laboratory test is specific or required. The clinician inspects and palpates the groove, assesses the degree of distal swelling, skin integrity, and vascular status of the toe, and asks about pain, duration, and barefoot walking history. Plain radiography is performed in all but early cases to evaluate the underlying bone: the progressive constriction remodels and eventually destroys the proximal phalanx, and X-ray findings are graded from a simple soft-tissue groove with an intact bone to a pencil-point phalanx with only a bony nubbin remaining. Doppler ultrasound or digital plethysmography can quantify residual blood flow and help decide between conservative surgery and amputation. MRI is rarely necessary but delineates soft-tissue extent and bone involvement when surgical planning is complex. Biopsy of the fibrous band is not needed for diagnosis but, when obtained, shows dense hypocellular fibrous tissue without granulomata, ruling out leprosy and systemic sclerosis. The critical task at diagnosis is distinguishing true ainhum from pseudoainhum, which requires examining the rest of the skin and obtaining a history of systemic disease.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Classify disease severity (groove depth, distal swelling, pain score, vascular status) to guide management decisions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician inspects both feet, measures the groove circumference, assesses toe perfusion by capillary refill, and documents deformity; no discomfort beyond the examination itself.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluate bone involvement and classify the stage of phalangeal destruction caused by the constricting band.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard weight-bearing or AP X-rays of the forefoot; no preparation needed and takes only a few minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measure residual arterial flow in the fifth toe to determine whether the digit is salvageable.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld probe is placed over the toe while you lie still; the test is painless and takes 15–20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Skin biopsy of the fibrous band
diagnosticTests.purpose: Exclude pseudoainhum causes such as leprosy or systemic sclerosis when the clinical picture is uncertain.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small punch biopsy under local anaesthesia; the sample is sent to histopathology; mild soreness for a few days afterwards.

diagnosticTests.test: Serology and skin smears for leprosy
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rule out Mycobacterium leprae as the cause of digital fibrosis, especially in high-endemic regions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample and/or slit-skin smear from the band site examined under microscopy; results in 1–7 days depending on laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provide detailed soft-tissue and bone marrow imaging when radiography is inconclusive or surgical planning requires precise anatomical detail.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in a scanner for 30–45 minutes; no radiation; the foot is positioned in a dedicated coil.

treatmentOverview: Treatment strategy is dictated by the stage of disease at presentation. In early ainhum — when the groove is shallow, the bone is intact, and vascular supply is preserved — a Z-plasty or linear incision with release of the fibrous band is highly effective, relieving pain and halting progression in the majority of patients. The procedure is performed under local or regional anaesthesia as a day-case operation. In moderate disease, where the proximal phalanx has been partially remodelled, the same surgical release is attempted but often needs to be combined with bone-end trimming to allow wound closure without tension. In advanced ainhum, where only a narrow fibrous pedicle connects the digit and bone has been almost entirely resorbed, surgical amputation of the pedunculated toe through the phalanx remnant gives the most reliable pain relief and functional outcome, with rapid healing. Watchful waiting is not appropriate once pain is significant or vascularity is threatened, because the condition does not arrest spontaneously. Footwear modification — wide, cushioned shoes or custom orthotics to redistribute plantar pressure — is used as an adjunct in early cases and as the sole management when surgery is declined. Topical keratolytics and emollients have no effect on the fibrous process itself but can relieve skin thickening over the band. Pain management with simple oral analgesics, including NSAIDs, provides interim relief while awaiting surgery. Antibiotics are required if secondary bacterial infection of the groove supervenes.

medicalTreatments.name: Footwear modification and orthotics
medicalTreatments.description: Wide-fitting shoes with a soft upper, padded insoles, and sometimes custom orthotics are prescribed to reduce lateral and plantar pressure on the fifth toe, slowing fibrous band progression and alleviating pain in mild cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for symptom relief in up to 60–70% of early-stage patients; does not reverse established fibrosis but delays surgical need.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs and analgesics
medicalTreatments.description: Oral non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (e.g. ibuprofen, naproxen) and simple analgesics are used for pain control during the conservative phase or while awaiting surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provide meaningful pain reduction in approximately 50–60% of patients with mild-to-moderate pain; no effect on the fibrous process.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical keratolytics (salicylic acid, urea)
medicalTreatments.description: Applied to the skin overlying the band to reduce keratinisation and skin thickening, improving comfort and groove hygiene.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest benefit for skin symptoms in an estimated 40–50% of users; no impact on underlying fibrosis.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotic therapy for secondary infection
medicalTreatments.description: When the groove becomes macerated and infected, a course of oral antibiotics targeted at skin organisms (e.g. flucloxacillin, co-amoxiclav) is needed to prevent spread to bone.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective in resolving superficial infection in the vast majority of cases when started early; does not treat the ainhum itself.

surgicalOptions.name: Z-plasty release of the fibrous band
surgicalOptions.description: The standard first-line surgical procedure for early and moderate ainhum: the constricting band is divided through a Z-shaped incision that redistributes skin tension, relieves the groove, and closes primarily without requiring skin grafting.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Greater than 80% success in relieving pain and halting progression in early-stage disease; recurrence requiring re-operation occurs in approximately 10–20% of cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Simple linear incision and fibrous band excision
surgicalOptions.description: In very early ainhum a straight incision over the groove with excision of the fibrous tissue is performed; simpler than Z-plasty but carries a higher recurrence rate.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 60–70% long-term success; best for shallow bands with minimal distal swelling.

surgicalOptions.name: Amputation of the fifth toe
surgicalOptions.description: For advanced ainhum where the toe is pedunculated, bone has been largely resorbed, and vascular compromise is severe, controlled amputation through the proximal phalanx remnant is the definitive treatment, giving reliable pain relief and a healed stump.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Healing is achieved in more than 90% of cases; patients consistently report significant improvement in quality of life and ability to wear normal shoes.

alternativeTreatments.name: Custom foot orthoses and pressure offloading
alternativeTreatments.description: Podiatry-fabricated insoles designed to redistribute load away from the lateral forefoot can slow progression and reduce pain as a non-surgical option.

alternativeTreatments.name: Traditional remedies (empirical data lacking)
alternativeTreatments.description: In communities where ainhum is endemic, traditional healers apply plant-derived preparations to the groove; no controlled evidence supports efficacy, and some preparations may cause contact dermatitis that worsens local inflammation.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist should assess any groove developing around the fifth toe to confirm true ainhum and exclude pseudoainhum causes such as leprosy and systemic sclerosis, which require entirely different management. A plastic surgeon, podiatric surgeon, or orthopaedic foot specialist is needed once the groove is deepening, the toe is swelling, or pain is affecting daily walking. Early specialist involvement dramatically increases the chance of saving the digit, because Z-plasty is most effective before bone remodelling begins.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a dermatologist with experience in rare fibrosing skin disorders, ideally one who has managed ainhum or pseudoainhum cases. For surgery, seek a plastic or podiatric surgeon who performs Z-plasty and digital procedures, with access to intraoperative Doppler assessment. In regions where ainhum is endemic, specialist tropical medicine clinics with combined dermatology-surgery services offer the most streamlined care. Ask whether the surgeon has managed late-stage ainhum cases and what their personal recurrence rate is after Z-plasty.
preventionStrategies: Wear properly fitting, wide-toed shoes that cushion the forefoot and protect the fifth toe from lateral compression.
Avoid prolonged barefoot walking on hard, uneven surfaces, particularly in high-risk individuals with a family history of ainhum.
Inspect the little-toe base weekly if you are at high risk, looking for any groove, skin thickening, or swelling.
Treat chronic fungal skin infections promptly to eliminate the inflammatory trigger around the toe groove.
Seek medical assessment immediately if a groove is noticed, before it deepens or bone involvement begins.
lifestyleModifications: Transition from barefoot to cushioned footwear as the primary lifestyle change with the strongest evidence for slowing progression.
Keep feet clean and dry; dry thoroughly between toes after washing to prevent fungal maceration at the groove site.
Apply moisturising emollients to the plantar surface daily to maintain skin suppleness and reduce friction over the band.
Use toe separators or silicone fifth-toe sleeves to reduce lateral ground pressure on the little toe.
Manage body weight if overweight, since each kilogram of excess weight adds proportionate load to the forefoot.
If diabetic, maintain strict glycaemic control to preserve digital circulation and wound-healing capacity.

recommended: A balanced diet rich in protein to support wound healing after surgery
Adequate vitamin C and zinc intake, both of which are required for normal collagen remodelling
Anti-inflammatory foods including oily fish, leafy vegetables, and nuts as a general vascular health measure
Sufficient hydration to maintain skin turgor and peripheral circulation
avoid: Smoking and tobacco use, which impair peripheral circulation and wound healing — a significant risk for post-surgical complications in ainhum
Excess alcohol, which can worsen peripheral neuropathy and mask pain that signals disease progression
A high-sugar diet if diabetic or pre-diabetic, given that poor glycaemic control accelerates peripheral vascular disease

exerciseGuidelines: Low-impact exercise such as swimming or cycling is well tolerated and keeps peripheral circulation healthy without further traumatising the plantar groove. Walking on cushioned surfaces with appropriate footwear is preferable to running or barefoot exercise. After Z-plasty or toe amputation, non-weight-bearing activity for two to four weeks is typical, followed by gradual return to walking with a protective surgical shoe. Full activity usually resumes by six to eight weeks post-operatively.
dailyManagement: Inspect both feet daily, noting any change in groove depth, toe swelling, skin colour, or pain intensity.
Wear padded, wide-fitting footwear at all times, even indoors, if the groove is present.
Clean and dry the groove gently each day to prevent maceration and secondary infection.
Take prescribed analgesics at consistent intervals rather than waiting until pain is severe.
Keep scheduled surgical follow-up appointments; do not delay if pain worsens suddenly.
prognosis: Without treatment, ainhum is progressive in virtually all cases, culminating in auto-amputation of the fifth toe over a period of 5–20 years from the onset of the groove. The toe is lost, but the process is otherwise self-limiting — the remaining foot is structurally unaffected. With early Z-plasty performed before significant bone destruction, more than 80% of patients retain a functional digit long term, with pain relief in the majority. Even after toe amputation, functional outcomes are excellent: most patients walk normally and experience no meaningful gait impairment from the loss of the fifth toe alone. The main risk to quality of life is delayed diagnosis; patients who present with a fully pedunculated or gangrenous toe have no option other than amputation. Secondary infection progressing to osteomyelitis is rare with prompt care but adds morbidity.
recoveryTimeline: After Z-plasty, the wound heals in two to three weeks with good technique and clean aftercare. Full weight-bearing with a cushioned shoe is usually possible by week three to four. Swelling in the released toe may persist for two to three months. After toe amputation, stump healing is generally complete in three to six weeks, and patients can return to ordinary footwear within six to eight weeks. Long-term review at six and twelve months is standard to detect recurrence of the fibrous band.
complications: Auto-amputation — the natural end-stage of untreated ainhum, leaving the patient without the fifth toe.
Secondary bacterial infection of the groove, which can progress to osteomyelitis if untreated and requires intravenous antibiotics or surgical debridement.
Wound dehiscence after Z-plasty, more common in patients who smoke or have poorly controlled diabetes, sometimes requiring re-operation.
Chronic phantom or residual pain after amputation in a minority of patients.
Recurrence of the fibrous groove after surgical release, occurring in approximately 10–20% of cases, necessitating repeat surgery or eventual amputation.
Gait imbalance and forefoot pain in the rare patient who loses additional digits or has bilateral involvement.

supportResources.name: WHO Neglected Tropical Diseases

supportResources.name: American Academy of Dermatology — Rare Skin Conditions
supportResources.description: Patient-focused information on rare dermatological diagnoses from the professional body for U.S. dermatologists.

sources.title: Browne SG. Ainhum: a clinical and aetiological study of 83 cases. Annals of Tropical Medicine and Parasitology, 1961
Dent DM et al. Ainhum and the pseudoainhum syndrome. South African Medical Journal, 1981

confusedWithConditions.name: Leprosy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leprosy causes digital fibrous bands as pseudoainhum via nerve damage and granulomatous inflammation; slit-skin smears and nerve thickening distinguish it from true ainhum.

confusedWithConditions.name: Systemic sclerosis (scleroderma)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Systemic sclerosis produces digital constriction bands as pseudoainhum alongside Raynaud phenomenon, sclerodactyly, and systemic features absent in true ainhum.

confusedWithConditions.name: Raynaud phenomenon
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Raynaud causes episodic colour changes and vascular spasm in multiple digits triggered by cold or stress, without a fixed fibrous groove.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriatic plaques can involve the toes and produce nail changes and skin thickening but do not cause a progressive constricting fibrous band.

relatedConditions.name: Systemic sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Produces pseudoainhum constriction bands indistinguishable clinically from true ainhum until systemic features are identified.

relatedConditions.name: Leprosy
relatedConditions.relevance: Nerve-damage-driven pseudoainhum is the most important differential in endemic regions and requires entirely different treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Raynaud phenomenon
relatedConditions.relevance: Digital ischemia in Raynaud disease can mimic the late vascular features of ainhum and may coexist in pseudoainhum due to systemic sclerosis.

relatedConditions.name: Peripheral artery disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Advanced ainhum-related digital ischemia overlaps clinically with severe peripheral artery disease and both are managed by vascular assessment before surgery.

faqs.question: What is ainhum?
faqs.answer: Ainhum is a rare condition where a fibrous band slowly constricts the base of the little toe, eventually causing the toe to fall off naturally if untreated. It occurs mainly in adult Black men of African descent.

faqs.question: Is ainhum painful?
faqs.answer: Yes. Most people with ainhum experience progressive pain in the little toe that worsens with walking and wearing shoes. In later stages the pain can be constant and severe as the blood supply to the toe is compromised.

faqs.question: What causes ainhum?
faqs.answer: The exact cause is not fully established, but a hereditary tendency toward excessive fibrosis combined with chronic barefoot walking on hard surfaces is the most widely accepted explanation. The fibrous band builds up over years in the plantar groove around the fifth toe.

faqs.question: Who gets ainhum?
faqs.answer: Ainhum affects predominantly adult Black men of West African descent, usually in their 40s and 50s. It is also reported in South Asian and some Brazilian populations. It is extremely rare in people of European descent.

faqs.question: Can ainhum be cured without surgery?
faqs.answer: In very early ainhum, wearing wide padded shoes reduces pressure and may slow the groove, but no non-surgical treatment reverses or stops the fibrous band once it is established. Surgery — either a Z-plasty incision or, in late cases, amputation — is the only curative option.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ainhum and pseudoainhum?
faqs.answer: True ainhum is an idiopathic fibrosing groove of the fifth toe in people of African descent with no systemic disease. Pseudoainhum is a similar-looking constriction caused by an underlying condition such as leprosy, systemic sclerosis, or keratoderma, and can affect any digit in any ethnic group.

faqs.question: How is ainhum diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical — a doctor inspects the characteristic groove around the little-toe base, checks blood flow in the toe, and takes an X-ray to see whether the underlying bone has been affected. Blood tests and a skin biopsy may be done to exclude leprosy or systemic sclerosis.

faqs.question: What surgery is done for ainhum?
faqs.answer: In early disease, a Z-plasty procedure — where the fibrous band is cut and the skin is rearranged — relieves the constriction and saves the toe. In advanced cases where blood flow is critically reduced and the bone has been largely destroyed, controlled amputation of the toe gives the best outcome.

faqs.question: Does losing the little toe affect walking?
faqs.answer: Loss of the fifth toe alone causes minimal functional impairment for most people. The little toe contributes to balance during the push-off phase of gait, but patients who undergo amputation for ainhum consistently report normal walking ability within weeks and no significant long-term gait problems.

faqs.question: Can ainhum come back after surgery?
faqs.answer: Recurrence of the fibrous groove after Z-plasty occurs in approximately 10 to 20% of patients, usually within the first two years. Continued use of protective footwear reduces this risk. Recurrent cases may need a repeat procedure or, if advanced, eventual toe amputation.

faqs.question: Is ainhum contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Ainhum is not infectious or contagious. It results from a combination of genetic susceptibility and local mechanical trauma, not from any microorganism that can spread between people.

faqs.question: What happens if ainhum is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Without treatment the fibrous band tightens progressively over 5 to 20 years until the blood supply is completely cut off and the toe separates naturally. The resulting wound usually heals but the toe is permanently lost.

faqs.question: Does wearing shoes prevent ainhum?
faqs.answer: Wearing well-fitting, cushioned shoes significantly reduces the mechanical stress that triggers groove formation and may slow or prevent progression in people who are genetically susceptible. It is the main preventive measure recommended in high-risk communities.

faqs.question: Can both feet be affected by ainhum?
faqs.answer: Bilateral ainhum is reported in approximately 10 to 20% of cases, though one side usually progresses faster. The condition almost always targets the fifth toe; involvement of other toes is rare in true ainhum.

faqs.question: How long does Z-plasty recovery take?
faqs.answer: The wound typically heals within two to three weeks. Most patients can walk with a cushioned protective shoe by week three or four and return to normal footwear and activity by six to eight weeks. Regular follow-up at six and twelve months checks for recurrence.

faqs.question: Is ainhum related to leprosy?
faqs.answer: They are different diseases but can look alike. Leprosy causes a pseudoainhum groove through nerve damage and is the most important differential in tropical regions. A skin smear for Mycobacterium leprae and assessment for nerve thickening quickly distinguishes the two.

faqs.question: What specialist should I see for ainhum?
faqs.answer: Start with a dermatologist to confirm the diagnosis and exclude systemic causes. If surgery is needed, a plastic surgeon or podiatric surgeon experienced in Z-plasty and digital procedures is the appropriate specialist.

faqs.question: Can children get ainhum?
faqs.answer: Ainhum in children under 20 is rare. When a groove appears in a child's toe, pseudoainhum from an underlying condition is far more likely and should be thoroughly investigated before a diagnosis of true ainhum is accepted.

faqs.question: Are there any medications that treat the fibrous band itself?
faqs.answer: No proven pharmacological agent dissolves or arrests the fibrous band in ainhum. Intralesional corticosteroids have been tried anecdotally with inconsistent results. Surgery remains the only reliably effective intervention for the fibrous band.

faqs.question: How common is ainhum worldwide?
faqs.answer: Ainhum is rare globally. Prevalence in susceptible West African communities is estimated at 0.02 to 2%. The overall global burden is uncertain because many cases in rural endemic areas are never formally diagnosed or reported.

metaDescription: Ainhum forms a fibrous band around the little toe, slowly causing auto-amputation. Learn symptoms, stages, Z-plasty surgery and prevention. Doctor-reviewed.
